data.title: Deutsche Bank Warns Of $30B Risk In Private Credit Holdings
data.description:
Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) warned in its annual report of a $30 billion exposure to private credit, highlighting growing concerns about lofty technology valuations, the sustainability of the current AI-driven capital spending cycle, and how these developments could affect business models and client credit risk.

The remainder is diversified across single and multi-asset lenders, Net Asset Value (NAV) Financing, Single Asset Financing, non-bank CRE lending, business development companies (BDC), and subscription finance.

During 2025, private credit and other non-bank financial institutions (NBFIs) faced pressure from higher interest rates, refinancing risks, and weak investor sentiment. Failures among several U.S. subprime lenders heightened scrutiny of underwriting standards and fraud risks in the sector.

The Frankfurt-based lender said its direct exposure to NBFIs is limited, though indirect exposure could arise through counterparties and related portfolios. In a worsening scenario—especially alongside geopolitical shocks—these risks could lead to weaker portfolio quality, higher credit losses, and increased capital and liquidity demands as clients draw on funding lines.

Despite the risks, Deutsche said it plans to accelerate infrastructure investment. The bank also aims to expand private credit across its corporate and investment banking divisions and broaden its regional distribution.

The asset manager plans to accelerate infrastructure investments and expand private credit with the corporate and investment banks. The bank also plans to widen its distribution through "selective regional expansion."

Other banks have issued warnings or restricted lending in their private credit portfolios recently amid the sector frenzy. 

Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley curbed redemptions after investors sought to withdraw nearly 11% of shares from its North Haven Private Income Fund. Adhering to its quarterly limit, the fund fulfilled only 45.8% of those requests. This

Blackstone permitted investors to withdraw a larger-than-normal sum of $3.7 billion from its $82 billion BCRED fund, according to a recent filing. After receiving $2 billion in new inflows, net redemptions totaled approximately $1.7 billion.

Meanwhile, Cliffwater's investor redemption requests from its $33 billion private credit flagship fund have exceeded 7%, Bloomberg reported.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Elon Musk Documentary To 'Explore His Motivations': Why Did He Buy Twitter And Join Forces With Trump?
data.description:
The world's richest billionaire Elon Musk has been the subject of movies and documentaries, with people wanting to know more about the CEO and entrepreneur. Musk is the subject of a new documentary that serves as a sequel to a series from 2022.

BBC Takes on Elon Musk, Again

Adding to the list of movies and documentaries being made about Musk, BBC is adding its name to the growing list with a follow-up title to its 2022 series "The Elon Musk Show.

Tentatively titled "Musk: The Next Chapter," the new project is expected to look into Musk's relationship with President Donald Trump, which included the launch of the Department of Government Efficiency and a public falling out that was later reversed.

The 72 Films documentary features interviews with people who are said to be close to Musk and provide in-depth looks into what has changed in his personal and professional life since 2022.

"Musk, a visionary who wants to save free speech, the ultimate humanitarian, or an unelected superpower willing to burn it all down for his own benefit?" The documentary is said to answer.

Fremantle, which owns a majority stake in 72 Films, will be the global distributor of the project.

"It is essential to explore his motivations and the people we've interviewed have given real insight into how Elon Musk became who he is today," director Gussy Sakula-Barry said.

Sakula-Barry said Musk has "a unique position of power" and a lot to say about our future, making the documentary important to highlight his life and motivations.

Elon Musk Big Budget Move in the Works

While several smaller documentary and low-budget movies have been made about Musk, Tesla and SpaceX, the largest project around the billionaire was announced several years ago.

A24 landed the rights to the Walter Isaacson biography about Musk that was released in September 2023.

Director Darren Aronofsky was named the director and producer of the project, a decision that was blessed by Musk publicly on social media.

There have been minimal updates on the film since Aronofsky was attached in November 2023, including no word on who will play Musk in the film.

Musk teased in 2025 that he should release an autobiography, updating the story of his life since the 2023 Isaacson book and highlighting his life in his own words.  

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much $100 Invested In IBM 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth Today
data.description:
IBM (NYSE:IBM) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 4.16% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 15.12%. Currently, IBM has a market capitalization of $231.52 billion.

Buying $100 In IBM: If an investor had bought $100 of IBM stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $201.67 today based on a price of $247.68 for IBM at the time of writing.

data.title: Apple Neo Gets A+ Rating For Students, A For Grandma: MKBHD Says 'Most Disruptive Product' From Company In 10+ Years
data.description:
If you've ever heard the expression "so easy even your grandma could do it," chances are you can think of emerging technology and the struggles that some grandparents or older people may have with new products. A new Apple Inc (NASDAQ:AAPL) product is getting the easy enough for grandma tag from a top tech reviewer.

MKBHD Reviews MacBook Neo

The first sub-$1,000 dedicated budget laptop from Apple is here, and it's getting strong reviews. Here's what tech reviewer MKBHD, aka Marques Brownlee, had to say about the Neo and his ratings for various users.

In a video review, MKBHD admits that the Neo is "even better than I expected."

The tech reviewer says the MacBook Neo is only $600, but is incredibly capable and compelling at that price.

Among the praise the reviewer gives the Neo, the reviewer says the new laptop is "potentially Apple's most disruptive product in the last 10+ years."

MKBHD says there is nothing fundamentally new or anything consumers haven't seen from MacBooks before in the Neo.

"This combination of things that is about to make the entire Windows and Chromebook laptop industry very nervous," MKBHD says.  

The review highlights how many of the Apple MacBook features are included in this cheaper model, which is great for many types of consumers, noting that around 90% of the device is the same machine as MacBooks but for hundreds of dollars less.

"Easiest laptop for Apple to make."

MKBHD also provides grades for different consumer groups based on the price, usability and features. With a price point of $600, the laptop does not come with the same memory or gaming capabilities of other laptops, hurting some of the ratings.

  • Students: A+ rating
  • College Students: A rating
  • Writers: A+ rating
  • Photographers: B- rating
  • Coders: B rating
  • Editors: C+ rating
  • Podcasters: B+ rating
  • Gamers: D+ rating
  • Grandma: A rating

The Neo scores highly across many consumer groups in the reviewer's rating system, including the highest mark for students.

"This laptop was literally made for you."

MKBHD also praises the word processing, email and video conferencing capabilities, which make the laptop great for writers. The cheaper Apple laptop scores lower for photographers due to its display and performance, and for coders due to its memory constraints.

The worst rating comes for gamers based on the small display and limited RAM, making the Neo only really good to play casual games on.

Apple Targets Students, Is Grandma Next?

An A rating for Grandma in MKBHD's review could create a new target market for Apple.

The new, more affordable laptop from Apple is likely geared towards students, with a starting price of $599, or $499 for education customers.

Tech investor Gene Munster highlighted the target market of students in a recent video and said the Neo could help the company get higher market share for students among laptop users.

"The new $AAPL MacBook Neo should add 0.5% to overall revenue for the next year," Munster said.

Munster said the new laptop likely "won't impact margins," but will increase overall revenue and expand Apple's reach in the education market.

The investor estimated that the Neo could add $2 billion in annual revenue for Apple's Mac segment. That segment had revenue of $34 billion in fiscal 2025. The increased total would represent around 6% growth from the Neo.

Munster said the target market for the Neo, which was initially just students, could be around 25 million in the U.S. alone. That market currently relies on cheaper Chromebooks but could switch to Apple products if they were more affordable.

The number of older people in the U.S. and globally also probably relies on Chromebooks, tablets, or their smartphones. Some of these people may not have any Apple products and the Neo could serve as the introductory product into the Apple ecosystem.

Photo: Shutterstock
